hook: You already know how to graph a line and write an equation in slope-intercept form — those are powerful tools. But what happens when a real-world situation changes rules halfway through, or when you need to model something that bounces back like an absolute value? This unit expands your linear toolkit so you can describe virtually any situation that changes at a constant rate, no matter how many twists it takes.
concept: Every topic in this unit is about understanding, reshaping, and applying functions that behave in a linear or piecewise-linear way — captured by the unifying idea of a *linear function and its transformations*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will sharpen your ability to identify which inputs a function accepts and which outputs it produces, and communicate precisely using notation like $f(x)$.
Master how adding, subtracting, or multiplying constants shifts, reflects, and stretches any parent function so you can graph entire families of functions quickly.
Apply transformation rules to the V-shaped parent function $f(x) = |x|$ to graph and interpret absolute value functions in any form.
You will learn to define, evaluate, and graph functions that use different rules on different parts of their domain, like a GPS that recalculates when you change roads.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Solving Absolute Value Equations and Inequalities
topic_roadmap.description: Solve equations and inequalities involving absolute value by recognizing that $|x| = c$ creates two cases, and interpret your solutions on a number line.
Apply everything you've built to construct and interpret linear models that represent authentic data and make predictions about real-world situations.
